concepts.title: Introduction to capacitors
concepts.conceptBody:
Capacitance

In practice we cannot handle free point charges or hold them fixed at desired positions. A practical way to handle a charge would be to put it on a conductor. Thus, one use of a conductor is to store electric charge (or electric potential energy). But every conductor has, its maximum limit of storing the electric charge or potential energy. Beyond that limitthe dielectric (or insulator) in which the conductor is placed becomes ionized. 

A capacitor is a device which can store more electric charge or potential energy compared to an isolated conductor.

Capacitors have a tremendous number of applications. In the flash light used by photographers the energy and charge stored in a capacitor are recovered quickly. In other applications the energy is released more slowly.

concepts.title: Capacitance of Conductors
concepts.conceptBody:
Capacitance of Conductor

When a charge $q$ is given a conductor it spreads over the outer surface of the conductor. The whole conductor comes to the same potential (say V). This potential V is directly proportional to the charge q, i.e. $\text{V}\propto \text{q}$

when the proportionality sign is removed a constant of proportionality $\frac{1}{\text{C}}$ comes in picture. Hence, $\text{V}=\frac{\text{q}}{\text{C}}$

or  $\text{C}=\frac{\text{q}}{\text{V}}$

Here, $\text{C}$ is called the capacitance of the conductor. The SI unit of capacitance is called one farad ( $1\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ F})$. One farad is equal to coulomb per volt $\left( 1\text{C}/\text{V} \right)$.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }1\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ F}=1$ farad $=1\text{C}/\text{V}=1$ coulomb/volt

Method of Finding Capacitance of a Conductor 

Give a charge $q$ to the conductor. Find potential on it due to charge $q$. This potential $\text{V}$ will obviously be a function of $q$ and finally find $q/\text{V}$, which is the desired capacitance $\text{C}$.

Capacitance of a spherical conductor 

When a charge $q$ is given to a spherical conductor of radius $R$, the potential on it is,

$\text{V}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{\text{q}}{\text{R}}$

From this expression we find that, $\frac{\text{q}}{\text{V}}=4\pi {{\in }_{0}}\text{R}=\text{C}$

Thus, capacitance of the spherical conductor is,

$\text{C}=4\pi {{\epsilon }_{0}}\text{R}$

From this expression we can draw the following conclusions.

(i) $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ C}\propto \text{R}$ or $\text{C}$ depends on $\text{R}$ only. Which we have already stated that $\text{C}$ depends on the dimensions of the conductor. Moreover if two conductors have radii ${{R}_{1}}$ and ${{R}_{2}}$ then,

$\frac{{{\text{C}}_{1}}}{{{\text{C}}_{2}}}=\frac{{{\text{R}}_{1}}}{{{\text{R}}_{2}}}$

(ii) Earth is also a spherical conductor of radius $\text{R}=6.4\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{10}^{6}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ m}$. The capacity of earth is therefore

or

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \text{C}=\left( \frac{1}{9\times {{10}^{9}}} \right)\left( 6.4\times {{10}^{6}} \right)\approx 711\times {{10}^{-6}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ F} \\ {} & \text{C}=711\mu \text{F} \\ \end{array}$

From here, we can see that farad is a large unit. As capacity of such a huge conductor is only $711\mu \text{F}$.

Energy stored in a charged conductor 

Work has to be done in charging a conductor against the force of repulsion by the already existing charges on it. The work is stored as a potential energy in the electric field of the conductor. Suppose a conductor of capacity $\text{C}$ is charged to a potential ${{\text{V}}_{0}}$ and let ${{\text{q}}_{0}}$ be the charge on the conductor at this instant. The potential of the conductor when (during charging) the charge on it was $\text{q}\left( <{{\text{q}}_{0}} \right)$ is,

$\text{V}=\frac{\text{q}}{\text{C}}$

Now work done in bringing a small charge dq at this potential is,

$\text{dW}=\text{Vdq}=\left( \frac{\text{q}}{\text{C}} \right)\text{dq}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ total work done in charging it from 0 to ${{\text{q}}_{0}}$ is,

$\text{W}=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{{{\text{q}}_{0}}}\text{dW}=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{{{\text{q}}_{0}}}\frac{\text{q}}{\text{C}}\text{dq}=\frac{1\text{q}_{0}^{2}}{2\text{C}}$

This work is stored as the potential energy,

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ U}=\frac{1}{2}\frac{\text{q}_{0}^{2}}{\text{C}}$

Further by using ${{\text{q}}_{0}}=\text{C}{{\text{V}}_{0}}$ we can write this expression also as,

$\text{U}=\frac{1}{2}\text{CV}_{0}^{2}=\frac{1}{2}{{\text{q}}_{0}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{V}}_{0}}$

In general if a conductor of capacity $\text{C}$ is charged to a potential $\text{V}$ by giving it a charge $\text{q}$, then

$\text{U}=\frac{1}{2}\text{C}{{\text{V}}^{2}}=\frac{1}{2}\frac{{{\text{q}}^{2}}}{\text{C}}=\frac{1}{2}\text{qV}$

concepts.title: Types of Capacitors
concepts.conceptBody:
Capacitor and its types

Capacitor

When two conductor are placed at a small separation (close to each other), then this combination is called capacitor.

$\begin{array}{l} {} & \text{Q}\propto \text{V} \\ {} & \text{Q}=\text{CV} \\ {} & ~\left( \text{C}=\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ capacitance }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \right) \\ \end{array}$

Factors on which capacitance of the arrangement depend

(i) Shape and size of the conductor

(ii) Relation orientation of the conductor

(iii) The medium in which they are placed

Types of Capacitor

(1) Parallel Plate Capacitor 

When two conducting plates are placed parallel to each other at a small separation, then this combination is called parallel plate capacitor.

$\begin{array}{l} \text{V} =\left( {{\text{V}}_{+}}-{{\text{V}}_{-}} \right) \\ {} =\left( \text{Ed} \right) \\ {} =\left( \frac{\sigma \text{d}}{{{\epsilon }_{0}}} \right)=\left( \frac{\text{Qd}}{{{\epsilon }_{0}}\text{ A}} \right) \\ \text{C} =\left( \frac{\text{Q}}{\text{V}} \right)=\frac{\text{Q}\left( {{\in }_{0}}\text{ A} \right)}{\text{Qd}}=\left( \frac{{{\epsilon }_{0}}\text{ A}}{\text{ d}} \right) \\ \end{array}$

$\therefore$ capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor $=\left( \frac{{{\in }_{0}}\text{ A}}{\text{ d}} \right)$ 

General method to find the capacitance

Give charge $\text{Q},-\text{Q}$ to each surface and then find the potential difference $\text{V}$ between the surface and use $\text{C}=\left( \frac{\text{Q}}{\text{V}} \right)$

(2) Spherical Capacitor 

When a hollow (or solid) spherical conductor is surrounded by another concentric hollow spherical conductor, placed at a separation, then this combination represents a spherical capacitor.

$\begin{array}{l}  \left( {{V}_{a}}-{{V}_{b}} \right) =-\mathop{\int }_{b}^{a}\vec{E}\cdot d\vec{r} \\ \left( {{V}_{a}}-{{V}_{b}} \right) =\mathop{\int }_{a}^{b}\left( \frac{KQ}{{{r}^{2}}} \right)\cdot dr \\ =KQ\left| \frac{-1}{r} \right|_{a}^{b}=KQ\left[ \frac{1}{a}-\frac{1}{b} \right] \\ =\frac{Q\left( b-a  \right)}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}ab} \\ \end{array}$

$\therefore \text{C}=\frac{\text{Q}}{\left( \text{V} \right)}=\frac{4\pi {{\epsilon }_{0}}\text{ab}}{\left( \text{b}-\text{a} \right)}$

Cases : 

(i) When $b\to \infty $ i.e. the inner conductor is an isolated sphere, then

$\text{C}=\left( 4\pi {{\in }_{0}}\text{a} \right)$

(ii) When $\text{d}=\left( \text{b}-\text{a} \right)<\text{a},\text{b}$

[i.e. the two conductors are nearly touching]

$\text{C}=\left( \frac{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}{{\text{a}}^{2}}}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ d}} \right)=\frac{\left( 4\pi {{\text{a}}^{2}} \right){{\in }_{0}}}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ d}}=\left( \frac{{{\in }_{0}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A}}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ d}} \right)$

then, this combination for $\text{d}=\text{b}-\text{a}<<\text{a}$, $\text{b}$ behaves as a parallel plate capacitor.

(3) Cylindrical Capacitor

When a hollow (or solid) cylindrical capacitor is surrounded by another cylindrical capacitor placed coaxially, then this combination is called cylindrical capacitor.

Here $(1>>a,b)$

$\begin{array}{l} \left( {{\text{V}}_{\text{a}}}-{{\text{V}}_{\text{b}}} \right) ~=-\mathop{\int }_{\text{b}}^{\text{a}}\vec{E}\cdot \text{d}\vec{r} \\ \left( {{\text{V}}_{\text{a}}}-{{\text{V}}_{\text{b}}} \right) ~=\mathop{\int }_{\text{a}}^{\text{b}}\frac{\lambda }{\left( 2\pi {{\in }_{0}} \right)\text{r}}\text{dr} \\ {} ~=\frac{\lambda }{2\pi {{\in }_{0}}}\text{ln}\left( \frac{\text{b}}{\text{a}} \right) \\ \text{C} =\left( \frac{\text{Q}}{{{\text{V}}_{\text{ab}}}} \right)=\frac{\lambda \text{L}}{\left( \frac{\lambda }{2\pi {{\in }_{0}}} \right)\text{ln}\left( \frac{\text{b}}{\text{a}} \right)} \\ \text{C} ~=\frac{2\pi {{\in }_{0}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ L}}{\text{ln}\left( \frac{\text{b}}{\text{a}} \right)} \\ \end{array}$
